title: Executive Order 11537—Amending the Selective Service Regulations Concerning the Ordering of Registrants for Induction

flags.justification: This expands the circumstances under which a registrant who refused a physical examination can be compelled to report for induction proceedings before medical eligibility is confirmed, though the underlying acceptability determination requirement is preserved.
The order explicitly preserves the substantive protection: 'he shall not be inducted until his acceptability has been satisfactorily determined.'
The triggering condition (refusal or failure to comply with a physical-examination order) is precisely specified.
This closes a potential avoidance pathway (declining to appear for a required physical exam) rather than creating a new incentive problem.
A standing regulatory amendment to an ongoing (at the time) Selective Service system.
The broader Selective Service appeals and review process for other aspects of classification and induction remains intact.
This concern is addressed under Flags 1 and 2.

Issued during the Vietnam War draft era, this amendment is directly responsive to a documented draft-resistance tactic (declining to appear for the required physical examination) and carries genuine substantive significance in that historical context.
No second agency's competing mandate is implicated.
No coverage population or exemption structure is created.

notes: Validation batch (negative). Structural weight: 2/14 = 14.3%. A genuinely mixed case: the amendment can be read either as closing a procedural loophole used by some draft resisters, or as creating a real, if narrow, due-process tension by allowing induction proceedings to begin before medical eligibility is confirmed. Both readings are noted here rather than adjudicated, consistent with this project's evenhanded treatment of contested historical policy.
